textInfo.title: Elevated C-Reactive Protein (CRP)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) level, a marker of inflammation in the body. The value is above the normal range of 0-5 mg/l.
textInfo.originalDescription: CRP: 10
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your C-reactive protein (CRP) level was 10 mg/L. This is higher than the normal range of 0-5 mg/L, which can indicate the presence of inflammation in your body. CRP is a protein made by the liver and its level rises in response to inflammation from various causes such as infection or injury.

textInfo.title: Primary Health Goal: Performance
textInfo.originalDescription: What’s the primary insight you’re hoping to gain from your check-up: Performance: I'm looking for recommendations to support a longer, healthier life
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient's primary goal is to understand how to optimize their physical or mental performance.

textInfo.title: High Transferrin Saturation
textInfo.medicalDescription: Transferrin Saturation: 51% (Reference: 17 - 45%). The value is above the normal range, indicating a higher than normal amount of iron is bound to transferrin.
textInfo.originalDescription: Transferrinsättigung: ↑ 51 %
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your transferrin saturation is 51%, which is higher than the normal range of 17% - 45%. This measurement indicates how much of your transferrin protein is being used to carry iron. A high value suggests there may be an excess of iron in your body.

textInfo.title: Elevated Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) is elevated at 33.9 pg, exceeding the normal range of 26.5-33.5 pg. This indicates that the average amount of hemoglobin in each red blood cell is higher than normal.
textInfo.originalDescription: mittl. korpuskuläres Hämoglobin: 33.9 *
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) level was 33.9 pg, which is slightly above the normal range of 26.5-33.5 pg. MCH measures the average amount of hemoglobin, the protein that carries oxygen, in each of your red blood cells. A high MCH can sometimes be associated with certain types of anemia or other conditions.

textInfo.title: Current Smoker
textInfo.medicalDescription: Patient reports being a current smoker for 5 years, with a daily consumption of 20 cigarettes (equivalent to 1 pack per day).
textInfo.originalDescription: Did you ever smoke, or use nicotine products?: Current smoker. How many years have you been smoking?: 5. On average, how many packs per day did you smoke? (1 pack = approx. 20 cigarettes or equivalent product): 20
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: You have reported that you are a current smoker. You've been smoking for 5 years and consume approximately 20 cigarettes (1 pack) per day. Smoking is a significant risk factor for numerous health conditions, including heart disease, stroke, and many types of cancer.

textInfo.title: Alcohol Consumption
textInfo.originalDescription: How many alcoholic beverages do you typically consume in a week?: 1-2 drinks per week
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient reports consuming 1-2 alcoholic drinks per week.

textInfo.title: Key Health Insight Goal
textInfo.originalDescription: Looking forward, what’s the key piece of knowledge or understanding about your health that you are most hoping to gain from your Ahead checkup?: How to optimise sleep and energy
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient is hoping to gain knowledge on how to optimize their sleep and energy levels.

textInfo.title: Signs of Inflammation/Infection
textInfo.medicalDescription: Elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) level at 22.2 mg/L, significantly above the normal range of 0-5 mg/L, indicating inflammation. Additionally, leukocyte count is slightly elevated at 10.2 G/l (normal range 4-10 G/l), with a corresponding elevation in absolute granulocytes (neutrophils) at 7.8 G/l (normal range 1.2-6.8 G/l). This pattern (leukocytosis with neutrophilia and high CRP) is suggestive of an acute inflammatory response, possibly due to infection.
textInfo.originalDescription: CRP: 22.2 *, Lc: 10.2 *, Granulozyten absolut: 7.8*
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your blood test showed several signs of inflammation. Your C-Reactive Protein (CRP) was high at 22.2 mg/L (normal is below 5). Your white blood cell count (Leukocytes) and a specific type of white blood cell called Granulocytes were also elevated. This combination suggests your body was fighting an infection or had some other form of inflammation at the time of the test.

textInfo.title: Low Transferrin Level
textInfo.medicalDescription: Transferrin: 21.4 µmol/l (Reference: 27.1 - 46.0 µmol/l). The value is below the normal range.
textInfo.originalDescription: Transferrin: ↓ 21.4 μmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your transferrin level is 21.4 µmol/l, which is below the normal range of 27.1 - 46.0 µmol/l. Transferrin is a protein that binds to iron and transports it through the blood. Low levels can sometimes be associated with certain types of anemia or liver conditions.

textInfo.title: Elevated Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) is elevated at 357 g/L, exceeding the normal range of 315-350 g/L. This indicates a higher than normal concentration of hemoglobin within the red blood cells.
textInfo.originalDescription: mittl. korpuskuläres Hämoglobin Konz.: 35.7*
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) was 357 g/L, which is above the normal range of 315-350 g/L. MCHC measures the concentration of hemoglobin inside your red blood cells. Elevated levels can sometimes be seen in certain types of anemia or other conditions affecting red blood cells.

textInfo.title: Family History of High Blood Pressure
textInfo.originalDescription: Has anyone in your close family members diagnosed with one of the following diseases?: High Blood Pressure. Who: Mother . At what age: 50
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient reports a family history of high blood pressure in their mother, who was diagnosed at age 50.

textInfo.title: Dietary Pattern
textInfo.originalDescription: Do you follow any specific dietary pattern?: No dietary restrictions - I eat all food groups
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient reports having no dietary restrictions and eats all food groups.

textInfo.title: Low-Normal Chloride Level
textInfo.medicalDescription: Chloride level is at the lower limit of the normal range (95-110 mmol/L).
textInfo.originalDescription: Chlorid: 94
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your chloride level was 94 mmol/L, which is at the very bottom of the normal range of 95-110 mmol/L. Chloride is an important electrolyte that helps maintain fluid balance and blood pressure. A value at this level is generally not a cause for concern but reflects the lower end of normal body function.

textInfo.title: Sports Activities
textInfo.originalDescription: Please describe your typical sport activities: Going for walk, Running, Other. Please specify: Football and tennis mainly
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient reports their typical sports activities include going for walks, running, football, and tennis.

textInfo.title: Ancestry
textInfo.originalDescription: Ancestry and ethnic background can be relevant for certain genetic conditions, medication responses, and laboratory reference ranges. (Select all that apply to your family's ancestral background): European (including Mediterranean)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient reports having a European (including Mediterranean) ancestral background.

textInfo.title: Low Uric Acid Level
textInfo.medicalDescription: Uric acid: 193 µmol/l (Reference: 230 - 480 µmol/l). The value is below the normal range.
textInfo.originalDescription: Harnsäure: ↓ 193 μmol/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your uric acid level is 193 µmol/l, which is below the normal range of 230 - 480 µmol/l. Uric acid is a waste product found in the blood. While high levels are more commonly a concern (associated with gout), very low levels can sometimes be linked to other health conditions and may warrant further evaluation.

textInfo.title: Slightly Elevated Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) is slightly elevated at 33.8 pg, just above the normal range of 26.5-33.5 pg.
textInfo.originalDescription: mittl. korpuskuläres Hämoglobin: 33.8*
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) level was 33.8 pg, which is slightly above the normal range of 26.5-33.5 pg. MCH measures the average amount of hemoglobin in each red blood cell. A value just above the normal range is usually not clinically significant on its own.

textInfo.title: Low-Normal Absolute Monocyte Count
textInfo.medicalDescription: Absolute monocyte count is at the lower limit of the normal range (0.3-0.8 10E9/l).
textInfo.originalDescription: Monozyten absolut: 0.3*
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your absolute monocyte count was 0.3 x10^9/L, which is at the very bottom of the normal range of 0.3-0.8 x10^9/L. Monocytes are a type of white blood cell that helps fight infection. A value at this level is generally not a cause for concern but reflects the lower end of the normal range.

textInfo.title: Elevated Granulocyte Count
textInfo.medicalDescription: Absolute granulocyte count is elevated at 7.8 G/l (normal range 1.2-6.8 G/l). This neutrophilia, along with elevated CRP and leukocytosis, strongly suggests an acute inflammatory or infectious process.
textInfo.originalDescription: Granulozyten absolut: 7.8*
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your absolute granulocyte count was 7.8 G/L, which is higher than the normal range of 1.2-6.8 G/L. Granulocytes are a type of white blood cell that fights infection. An elevated count is a common sign that your body is dealing with an infection or inflammation.

textInfo.title: Medication: Concerta
textInfo.originalDescription: Is there anything else you feel would be helpful for us to know about your health or lifestyle?: I have mild adhd and I take pills (concerta - 18mg)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient reports taking Concerta (18mg), presumably for their reported mild ADHD.

textInfo.title: Slightly Elevated White Blood Cell Count
textInfo.medicalDescription: Leukocyte count is slightly elevated at 10.2 G/l, just above the upper normal limit of 10 G/l. This is consistent with other inflammatory markers from the same date.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your white blood cell count (Leukocytes or 'Lc') was 10.2 G/L, which is slightly above the normal range of 4-10 G/L. White blood cells are part of your immune system, and a slight elevation can be a sign that your body is responding to something, like a minor infection or inflammation.

textInfo.title: Medical History: Mild ADHD
textInfo.originalDescription: Is there anything else you feel would be helpful for us to know about your health or lifestyle?: I have mild adhd and I take pills (concerta - 18mg)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient reports a history of mild ADHD.

textInfo.title: Self-Rated Diet Health
textInfo.originalDescription: On a scale of 1 (unhealthy) to 10 (very healthy), how would you rate your diet?: 8
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: On a scale from 1 (unhealthy) to 10 (very healthy), the patient rates their diet as an 8.

textInfo.title: Sleep Quality
textInfo.originalDescription: How would you generally describe your sleep quality?: Generally good, sometimes a bit restless
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient describes their sleep quality as generally good, though sometimes a bit restless.

textInfo.title: High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) Elevated
textInfo.medicalDescription: hs-CRP: 3.9 mg/l (Reference: <1.0 mg/l for low risk). This value falls into the high-risk category for coronary heart disease (3.1 - 10.0 mg/l), suggesting a significant level of inflammation in the body.
textInfo.originalDescription: CRP sensitiv: ↑ 3.9 mg/l
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) level is 3.9 mg/l. According to the lab, this places you in a high-risk category for coronary heart disease, as it is above the 3.0 mg/l threshold. hs-CRP is a marker of inflammation in the body, and elevated levels can be associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular events.

textInfo.title: Activity Level
textInfo.originalDescription: Let's talk about your activity levels. How often do you typically engage in physical exercise, and what does your usual routine look like?: Very active: Intense exercise or sports, 5 or more hours per week of moderate activity or 2.5-3h of intense sport.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient describes their activity level as 'Very active', which includes intense exercise or sports, 5 or more hours per week of moderate activity, or 2.5-3 hours of intense sport.

textInfo.title: High Percentage of Granulocytes
textInfo.medicalDescription: Relative granulocyte count is elevated at 76.6% (normal range 43-76%). This finding supports the presence of neutrophilia.
textInfo.originalDescription: Granulozyten %: 76.6*
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The percentage of granulocytes in your white blood cells was 76.6%, which is slightly above the normal range of 43-76%. This means that a higher proportion of your white blood cells were granulocytes, which often happens when the body is fighting an infection.

textInfo.title: High C-Reactive Protein (CRP)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) level, a marker of inflammation in the body. The value is significantly above the normal range of 0-5 mg/l.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your C-reactive protein (CRP) level was 26.9 mg/L. This is significantly higher than the normal range of 0-5 mg/L, which indicates a notable level of inflammation in your body. CRP is a protein made by the liver and its level rises in response to inflammation from various causes such as infection or injury.

textInfo.title: List of Supplements
textInfo.originalDescription: Are you currently taking any vitamins, minerals, or other dietary supplements?: Yes. Supplements: Magnesium, Multivitamin, Omega 3
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient reports taking the following supplements: Magnesium, Multivitamin, and Omega 3.

textInfo.title: Self-Rated Stress Level
textInfo.originalDescription: On a scale of 1 (low stress) to 10 (high stress), how would you generally rate your stress levels over the past few months?: 5
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: On a scale from 1 (low stress) to 10 (high stress), the patient rates their stress level as a 5.

textInfo.title: Slightly Elevated Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) is slightly elevated at 352 g/L, just above the normal range of 315-350 g/L.
textInfo.originalDescription: mittl. korpuskuläres Hämoglobin Konz.: 35.2 *
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) was 352 g/L, which is slightly above the normal range of 315-350 g/L. MCHC measures the concentration of hemoglobin inside your red blood cells. A result just over the normal range is often not a cause for concern.

textInfo.title: Average Sleep Duration
textInfo.originalDescription: On average, about how many hours of sleep do you get per night?: 7
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The patient reports getting an average of 7 hours of sleep per night.
